reference_answer: PRESCOTT, WI. (Stated in the dateline at the start of both the lede paragraph and the section-heading restatement of the lede.)
March 18, 2026. (Stated as a standalone line between the first restatement of the lede and the section-heading restatement.)
California. (Paragraph 2, which describes the employer as "California-based SV Labs Prescott Corp." The incident location, by contrast, is Prescott, WI.)
$275,694. (Last sentence of paragraph 3: "The agency proposed penalties of $275,694.")
Nitrocellulose. (Lede paragraph: "a 55-gallon fiber drum containing nitrocellulose exploded.")
26-210-CHI. (Last line of the document, after the contacts block.)

The release number suffix "CHI" denotes OSHA's Chicago regional office. The document itself does not spell this out; the inference rests on standard OSHA release-number conventions, so this is a partially qualified answer — the document supports "CHI" but does not define it.
In paragraph 2 only, where the company is described as "California-based SV Labs Prescott Corp." The headline, dateline, and section heading all reference only the incident-site state (Wisconsin).
Paragraph 2: it states OSHA "initiated an inspection into California-based SV Labs Prescott Corp. after the explosion and subsequent fire."
Paragraph 4: "The employer has contested the citations before the independent Occupational Safety and Health Review Commission."

- Juan J. Rodríguez, 972-850-4709 - Eric R. Lucero, 678-237-0630 - Erika B. Ruthman, 678-237-0630 Note: Lucero and Ruthman are listed with the same phone number in this release. Whether that is a transcription error or a shared line is not stated.

Per paragraph 3, the 10 serious violations cover: - flammable liquids; - fire extinguisher training and inspections; - forklift training and inspections; - electrical hazards; - hazard communications. The release does not break out how many citations fall in each subcategory.
Per paragraph 3: - recordkeeping violations; - lack of powered industrial truck training; - unsafe material handling.
Respiratory protection; and hazard communication. (Paragraph 3.)
- rodriguez.juan@dol.gov - lucero.eric.r@dol.gov - ruthman.erika.b@dol.gov

Sequence per the release text: 1. A 55-gallon fiber drum containing nitrocellulose explodes (lede). 2. The explosion starts a secondary fire involving flammable liquids (lede). 3. OSHA initiates an inspection (paragraph 2). 4. OSHA finds the employer lacks a new-hire safety and health program and failed to provide chemical and respiratory protection training, fit testing, and medical evaluations (paragraph 2). 5. OSHA cites the employer for two repeat, ten serious, and six other-than-serious violations and proposes $275,694 in penalties (paragraph 3). 6. The employer contests before the OSHRC (paragraph 4). The release does not give specific calendar dates for any of these steps; only the news-release issuance date (March 18, 2026) is dated.
No. The release says only that OSHA "initiated an inspection ... after the explosion and subsequent fire." Neither the explosion date nor the inspection-opening date appears in the document.
No. Abatement deadlines are not mentioned. The release notes only that "Penalties and citations may be adjusted throughout the course of the case process."
No. The release uses present-perfect framing ("has contested") and gives no filing date.

Per paragraph 2, OSHA determined the employer: - did not have a thorough new hire safety and health program in place; - failed to provide newly hired employees with chemical and respiratory protection training; - failed to provide newly hired employees with respirator fit testing; - failed to provide newly hired employees with medical evaluations.
18 citations total: 2 repeat + 10 serious + 6 other-than-serious. The release does not give this total directly; it is derivable by adding the three category counts in paragraph 3.

Contested. Paragraph 4 states the employer has contested the citations before OSHRC and that "Penalties and citations may be adjusted throughout the course of the case process." Readers are directed to "check the OSHA establishment search page periodically for any changes." The release thus explicitly disclaims that the cited counts and penalty figure are final.

The body calls the company "California-based SV Labs Prescott Corp." (paragraph 2). The headline's "Wisconsin" reflects the incident-site location (Prescott, WI) rather than the company's corporate domicile. Both characterizations are accurate but they describe different things: the headline locates the event, the body locates the corporation. A reader who treats "Wisconsin beauty products manufacturer" as a corporate-domicile claim would be misled.

The inspection was triggered by a nitrocellulose drum explosion and a secondary fire involving flammable liquids. The findings in paragraph 3 that most directly map to that cause are: the two repeat violations covering respiratory protection and hazard communication (relevant to chemical safety around nitrocellulose and other flammables); and the serious violations covering flammable liquids, fire extinguisher training and inspections, electrical hazards, and hazard communications. The forklift and powered-industrial-truck items are less directly tied to the cited incident cause. The release does not explicitly map any single citation to the explosion event.

Within this document, Lucero and Ruthman are listed with the same phone number (678-237-0630), which is suspicious on its face: it could be a shared press line, but more commonly each named OSHA media contact has a distinct direct number. Cross-document check: in OSHA's national news release on the Yarmouth, MA cave-in (Release 26-574-ATL, April 1, 2026), Ruthman is listed with 678-237-0631 — a one-digit difference. That suggests the value in this Chicago-office release is most likely a typo (the trailing "0" should be "1"), but the document itself does not concede this. The honest answer here is qualified: the numbers in this document are internally suspicious; cross-source evidence from another OSHA release suggests Ruthman's correct number is 678-237-0631, not 678-237-0630; but this single document does not resolve the discrepancy.
